message.content:
You are a technical fact-checker with web access. Read the design document at /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jleechan-projects-worktree-runner/8ca589dd-8422-44b1-a543-52bd7b71f14b/scratchpad/gistfile1.txt (a design for "gha-isolated", a Rust CLI wrapping "gha-outrunner" for isolated self-hosted GitHub Actions runners). Use WebSearch/WebFetch extensively to verify EVERY external factual assumption in the design. Specifically investigate:
1. Does a project called "gha-outrunner" actually exist? Search GitHub, crates.io, web. If it does not exist or is obscure/unmaintained, that is a CRITICAL finding — the whole design wraps it. Check if the author may have meant something else (e.g. actions-runner-controller, github-runner projects, "runs-on", ...).
2. Tart (tart.run / cirruslabs/tart) licensing: is it free for this use? (Fair Source / paid tiers — what are the actual limits as of 2026?)
3. GitHub "Scale Sets": runner scale sets are an Actions Runner Controller (ARC / Kubernetes) concept — can a standalone CLI use scale sets without k8s? Or should the design say "ephemeral runners + JIT config" instead?
4. GCE nested virtualization: the design claims GCE has "no strong KVM" — but GCE supports nested virtualization on most x86 machine types. Verify.
5. Sysbox status in 2026: Docker acquired Nestybox; is Sysbox CE maintained? Kernel/distro requirements?
6. serde_yaml crate: deprecated/archived? What's the recommended replacement?
7. Any well-established existing tools that already do what gha-isolated proposes (e.g. cirruslabs gitlab/github runner tart executors, RunsOn, actuated, garm (Cloudbase GitHub Actions Runner Manager), ARC, ubicloud, warpbuild, etc.) — is this reinventing an existing wheel?
Report each as a finding with evidence URLs. Be precise about what you could and could not verify.

content.text: Now let me verify each claim with web searches, batching independent queries.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about Tart's licensing:

Both Tart Virtualization and Orchard Orchestration are licensed under Fair Source License. The Free Tier license has a 100 CPU core limit for Tart and 4 Orchard Workers limit for Orchard.

For paid tiers, organizations that wish to exceed the limits of the Platinum Tier license can purchase a custom Diamond Tier License, which costs $12 per CPU core per year and gives the ability to run unlimited Orchard Workers.

Additionally, usage on personal computers including personal workstations is royalty-free, but organizations that exceed a certain number of server installations (100 CPU cores for Tart and/or 4 hosts for Orchard) will be required to obtain a paid license.

Note: The search results indicate that as of April 2026, Cirrus Labs plans to relicense all of their source-available tools, including Tart, under a more permissive license.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about **gha-outrunner**:

## Overview

gha-outrunner provides ephemeral GitHub Actions runners without requiring Kubernetes, provisioning fresh Docker containers or VMs for each GitHub Actions job and then destroying them when the job completes.

## Key Features

- It uses GitHub's scaleset API to register as an autoscaling runner group.
- It provides isolation guarantees with Docker, libvirt, or Tart.
- No additional orchestrator is needed.

## Comparison to ARC

Unlike GitHub's Actions Runner Controller (ARC) which requires Kubernetes, gha-outrunner is designed for environments running on bare metal or a simple VPS without needing a cluster.

The project is maintained by NetwindHQ and can be found at https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner. It offers a lightweight alternative to Kubernetes-based runner orchestration solutions.

Here are the search results for GCE nested virtualization support with KVM and machine types:

## Overview
Nested virtualization lets you run virtual machine (VM) instances inside of other VMs so you can create your own virtualization environments. To support nested virtualization, Compute Engine adds Intel Virtualization Technology (VT-x) instructions to VMs, so when you create a VM, the hypervisor that is already on that VM can run additional VMs.

## Hypervisor Support
Compute Engine VMs run on a physical host that has Google's security-hardened, KVM-based hypervisor. The only hypervisor supported in an L1 VM is Linux KVM; Microsoft Hyper-V isn't supported.

## Machine Type Requirements
The VM needs an Intel Haswell or later CPU platform. E2 VMs, memory-optimized VMs, AMD or Arm based VMs, and H4D VMs are not supported.

## Enabling Nested Virtualization
When creating a VM with the --enable-nested-virtualization flag, this tells GCE to enable the VMX flag, which is what KVM needs to function.

## Scope of Support
The only supported nested virtualization is with the lower-level Compute Engine directly, which requires managing the VM ourselves. Cloud Run, Batch, and GKE do not expose the KVM device to containers.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about GitHub Actions runner scale sets and the standalone alternatives to Kubernetes-based Actions Runner Controller:

## GitHub Actions Runner Scale Sets Overview

Actions Runner Controller (ARC) is a Kubernetes operator that orchestrates and scales self-hosted runners for GitHub Actions, allowing you to create runner scale sets that automatically scale based on the number of workflows running in your repository, organization, or enterprise.

## Standalone Alternative: GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set Client

For organizations that don't want to use Kubernetes, there's a standalone option: The GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set Client is a standalone Go-based module that empowers platform teams, integrators, and infrastructure providers to build custom autoscaling solutions for GitHub Actions runners across VMs, containers, on-premise infrastructure, and cloud services, with support for Windows, Linux, and macOS platforms.

The client orchestrates GitHub API interactions for scale sets while leaving infrastructure provisioning to you. You define how runners are created, scaled, and destroyed, and configure runners with multiple labels for flexible job routing and targeting.

The Runner Scale Set Client is not a replacement for Actions Runner Controller (ARC), which remains the reference implementation of the scale set APIs and the recommended Kubernetes solution for autoscaling runners. Instead, the client is a complementary tool for interfacing with the same scale set APIs to build custom autoscaling solutions outside of Kubernetes.

The standalone client source code and documentation are available in the actions/scaleset repository which contains the complete source code, comprehensive documentation, and practical examples to help you get started, including implementation guides, sample configurations for various infrastructure scenarios, and reference architectures.

content.content:
# gha-outrunner Project Overview

## What It Does
Ephemeral GitHub Actions runners that provision fresh Docker containers or VMs for each job, then destroy them afterward. As stated in the project: "outrunner provisions fresh Docker containers or VMs for each GitHub Actions job, then destroys them when the job completes."

## Supported Backends
The project supports three provisioning backends:
- **Docker**: Linux containers (Linux/macOS host)
- **libvirt**: KVM VMs from qcow2 images (Linux host, Windows/Linux VMs)
- **Tart**: VM clones on Apple Silicon (macOS host, macOS/ARM64 Linux VMs)

## GitHub Integration
Yes, it "uses GitHub's scaleset API to register as an autoscaling runner group."

## Language & Repository Stats
- **Language**: Go (95.9%)
- **Stars**: 3
- **Forks**: 0
- **Latest Release**: v1.2.0 (April 20, 2026)
- **Total Releases**: 5

## Maintenance & License
- **License**: MIT
- Repository shows active development with recent releases and 17 open issues
- **Author**: Built by Paweł Subocz at Netwind

## Configuration & CLI
The documentation references setup guides and CLI/configuration references, though the exact config filename and specific CLI commands aren't detailed in the provided content. Documentation mentions "CLI reference" and "Configuration reference" available in the docs directory.

## Key Distinction
Unlike GitHub's Actions Runner Controller (ARC), outrunner doesn't require Kubernetes, making it suitable for bare metal or VPS deployments.

The enterprise version of the open-source Sysbox \"runc\" runtime (empowers rootless containers to run workloads such as Systemd, Docker, Kubernetes, just like VMs). · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/docker-archive/nestybox.sysbox-ee"},{"title":"Sysbox 0.7.0 not working on ubuntu 26.04 · Issue #1005 · nestybox/sysbox","url":"https://github.com/nestybox/sysbox/issues/1005"},{"title":"Can not run with sysbox-runc runtime · Issue #882 · nestybox/sysbox","url":"https://github.com/nestybox/sysbox/issues/882"},{"title":"Releases · nestybox/sysbox","url":"https://github.com/nestybox/sysbox/releases"},{"title":"nestybox/sysbox-in-docker - Docker Image","url":"https://hub.docker.com/r/nestybox/sysbox-in-docker"},{"title":"Issues · nestybox/sysbox","url":"https://github.com/nestybox/sysbox/issues"}]

Based on the search results, here's the status of Sysbox in 2026:

## Current Maintenance Status

Docker is the main sponsor of the Sysbox project, though Sysbox remains a community open-source project and is not officially supported by Docker. Support is provided on a best effort basis via the Github repo or via the Sysbox Slack Workspace.

## Sysbox Community Edition (Sysbox-CE) Status

The Community Edition is actively maintained and not deprecated. The search results show active development with recent releases and ongoing bug fixes. As of March 2026, Sysbox version 0.7.0 was built and deployed, indicating the project is still being actively maintained.

## Sysbox Enterprise Edition (Sysbox-EE) Status

Sysbox Enterprise is no longer offered as a standalone product and has been incorporated into Docker Desktop. Docker plans to make some Sysbox Enterprise features available in Sysbox Community Edition.

The Sysbox-EE repository was planned to be phased out in mid 2023 after existing license agreements expired, and it now resides in the docker-archive repository.

## Summary

Sysbox Community Edition (Sysbox-CE) is **not EOL or deprecated** in 2026 — it continues to receive updates and maintenance. However, the Enterprise Edition is no longer sold or maintained as a separate product.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the serde_yaml crate deprecation and alternatives:

## Deprecation Status

As of version 0.9.34, the serde_yaml author David Tolnay is not planning to publish further versions as none of his projects have been using YAML for a long time, so he archived the GitHub repo and marked the crate deprecated in the version number. The project is no longer maintained.

## No Official Replacement

An official replacement isn't designated for those who still need to work with YAML, but crates.io has a number of reasonable-looking options available.

## Regarding Your Specific Query Terms

The search results don't mention **serde_yml** or **serde_yaml_ng** as officially endorsed replacements. The official guidance simply directs users to search the crates.io registry for YAML-related alternatives rather than recommending specific alternatives.

If you're looking for information about specific alternative crates like serde_yml or serde_yaml_ng, I'd recommend checking their crates.io pages and GitHub repositories directly to evaluate their maintenance status and compatibility with your use case.

Based on the search results, here's what was found about Cirrus Labs joining OpenAI:

## Key Announcement

Cirrus Labs has entered into an agreement to join OpenAI as part of the Agent Infrastructure team, announced on April 7, 2026.

## Relicensing Plans

In the coming weeks, Cirrus Labs will relicense all of their source-available tools, including Tart, Vetu and Orchard under a more permissive license. Additionally, they have stopped charging licensing fees for them.

## Future Maintenance and Support

Tart, Vetu, and Orchard will continue to exist as open-source tools, but the dedicated team that built and maintained them is moving on to other projects at OpenAI. However, if you self-hosted macOS VMs using Tart, those tools remain available and the community can continue to build on them.

## Business Changes

Cirrus CI is shutting down on June 1, 2026, and Cirrus Runners are closed to new customers, though existing contracts will be honored through their contract end dates.

The acquisition reflects OpenAI's focus on building infrastructure for AI agents, as agents that write code need places to safely run it, and tools like Tart create isolated sandboxes with resource limits and snapshots for state resets, which is exactly what you need when an AI is executing code autonomously.

Based on the search results, here's what I found about the GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set Client announcement:

## Overview

GitHub announced on February 5, 2026, that the GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set Client has entered public preview, enabling teams to build custom autoscaling solutions for self-hosted runners without requiring Kubernetes.

## Key Features

The client is a standalone Go-based module that lets you build custom autoscaling solutions for GitHub Actions runners without requiring Kubernetes, providing the core building blocks for implementing autoscaling on any infrastructure, whether you're running containers, virtual machines, bare metal servers, or cloud services.

The main capabilities include:
- Platform agnostic design: Works with containers, virtual machines, and bare metal servers across Windows, Linux, and macOS
- Full provisioning control: You define how runners are created, scaled, and destroyed based on your specific requirements
- Native multi-label support: Assign multiple labels to each scale set, optimizing resources for multiple build types
- Real-time telemetry: Monitor job execution and runner performance with built-in metrics

## Relationship to Actions Runner Controller (ARC)

The Runner Scale Set Client isn't a replacement for Actions Runner Controller (ARC), which remains a reference implementation of the scale set APIs and the recommended Kubernetes solution for autoscaling runners. Instead, the client is a new tool for interfacing with same scale set APIs for building custom autoscaling solutions outside of Kubernetes.

## Open Source Availability

This repository provides a standalone Go client for the GitHub Actions Runner Scale Set APIs, extracted from the actions-runner-controller project so that platform teams, integrators, and infrastructure providers can build their own custom autoscaling solutions for GitHub Actions runners.

Links: [{"title":"Ubicloud vs WarpBuild Comparison - WarpBuild Blog","url":"https://www.warpbuild.com/blog/ubicloud-warpbuild-comparison"},{"title":"GitHub - cloudbase/garm: Multi-cloud, auto-scaling manager for GitHub Actions & Gitea self-hosted runners with pluggable providers · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/cloudbase/garm"},{"title":"Fastest GitHub Actions Runners: CPU Speed - RunsOn","url":"https://runs-on.com/benchmarks/github-actions-cpu-performance/"},{"title":"GitHub Actions runners benchmark - RunsOn","url":"https://runs-on.com/benchmarks/github-actions-runners/"},{"title":"GitHub Actions Alternatives for Faster, Cheaper CI | RunsOn","url":"https://runs-on.com/alternatives-to/github-actions-runners/"},{"title":"12 Best Ubicloud Alternatives for GitHub Actions Runners | Better Stack Community","url":"https://betterstack.com/community/comparisons/ubicloud-alternatives/"},{"title":"12 Best Actuated Alternatives for GitHub Actions Runners | Better Stack Community","url":"https://betterstack.com/community/comparisons/actuated-alternatives/"},{"title":"Fastest GitHub Actions Runners: Disk I/O - RunsOn","url":"https://runs-on.com/benchmarks/github-actions-disk-performance/"}]

I've found comprehensive information comparing these GitHub Actions runner alternatives. Here's a summary of what I found:

## Overview of Alternatives

GARM is a multi-cloud, auto-scaling manager for GitHub Actions & Gitea self-hosted runners with pluggable providers.

## Key Provider Comparisons

**RunsOn vs Ubicloud:**
- RunsOn uses EC2 spot instances managed by open-source software in your AWS account, while Ubicloud uses Hetzner bare metal managed by a third party
- RunsOn and Ubicloud both land around 90% under GitHub-hosted pricing, with RunsOn deploying in your AWS account and Ubicloud backed by Hetzner

**Ubicloud vs WarpBuild:**
- Ubicloud's pricing is 10% of the cost of GitHub Actions runners for x86-64 and ~16% the cost for arm64 runners
- WarpBuild supports truly unlimited concurrency at no additional cost for x86-64 and arm64 instances
- Ubicloud runners are ephemeral VMs running on bare-metal, potentially subject to noisy neighbors and performance degradation

**CPU Performance:**
- On x64, Namespace, Blacksmith and RunsOn lead single-thread CPU performance; on arm64, Namespace leads, then RunsOn and WarpBuild

**Infrastructure Control:**
- RunsOn is the only solution entirely hosted in your AWS infrastructure, with no third-party or centralized service
- Actuated enables bring-your-own-hardware, and Cirun supports multi-cloud deployment

**Specializations:**
- RunsOn is the only alternative provider that currently offers GPU support
- RunsOn is the only alternative provider that currently offers Windows support


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

content.text: Let me verify gha-outrunner's actual config/CLI details and check the Tart licensing page details via search cache.

input.prompt: What is the config file name and format? List top-level config keys, how backends (docker/libvirt/tart) are configured, resource limit options (memory, cpus, pids_limit?), authentication (GitHub App/PAT), and any 'runners:' or 'count:' keys. Quote a minimal example.
List all CLI commands and flags. Is there start/stop/status? How is the daemon run (foreground, systemd)? Any service install command?

content.content:
# Outrunner Configuration Summary

## Config File
**Name & Format:** `config.yml` (YAML); default location `/etc/outrunner/config.yml`

## Top-Level Keys
- `url` — Global repository/org URL
- `token_file` — Path to GitHub PAT file
- `runners` — Map of scale set configurations

## Backend Configuration
Each runner in the `runners:` map specifies exactly one backend:

- **Docker:** `docker:` with `image` (required), `runner_cmd`, `mounts`
- **Libvirt:** `libvirt:` with `path` (required base qcow2), `runner_cmd`, `socket`, `cpus`, `memory`, `mount`
- **Tart:** `tart:` with `image` (required OCI/local), `runner_cmd`, `cpus`, `memory`, `mounts`

## Resource Limits
- `cpus` (int) — vCPU count; defaults vary by backend (typically 4)
- `memory` (int) — RAM in MiB; default 8192
- No `pids_limit` mentioned in documentation

## Authentication
Supports GitHub PAT via token resolution chain:
1. Per-runner `token_file`
2. `--token` CLI flag
3. `GITHUB_TOKEN` environment variable
4. systemd-creds path
5. Global `token_file`

*No GitHub App authentication documented.*

## Runner Keys
- `runners.<name>` — Scale set name; requires `labels` array and one backend
- `max_runners` — Concurrent runner limit (defaults to CLI `--max-runners`, default 2)
- No `count:` key mentioned

## Minimal Example
> "url: https://github.com/myorg/myrepo
> token_file: /etc/outrunner/token
> runners:
>   linux:
>     labels: [self-hosted, linux]
>     docker:
>       image: outrunner-runner:latest"

# CLI Commands and Flags

Based on the documentation, **outrunner has only one command** with the following flags:

| Flag | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `--config` | Specify config file path |
| `--url` | Set default repository/org URL |
| `--token` | Provide GitHub PAT |
| `--max-runners` | Set max concurrent runners (default: 2) |
| `-h, --help` | Display help |
| `-v, --version` | Show version |

## Missing Features

There are **no start/stop/status commands**. The tool operates as a single foreground process that "listens for jobs and provisions ephemeral runner environments for each one."

## Daemon Operation

- **Foreground execution**: Run `outrunner` directly; it blocks until interrupted
- **Shutdown**: Send SIGINT (Ctrl+C) for clean shutdown (exit code 0)
- **Systemd support**: The documentation mentions "systemd-creds" for credential storage, suggesting systemd integration is expected
- **No built-in service installer**: Users must manually create systemd service files

The tool maintains state across restarts—scale sets persist and are reused rather than recreated.

content.text: One more check — whether Tart's promised relicensing has actually landed.

input.prompt: What license is this? Is it Fair Source (FSL/Fair Core), Apache 2.0, MIT, or something else? Quote the license name and any usage limitation (e.g., CPU core limits, competing use).

content.content:
# License Analysis

**License Name:** Functional Source License, Version 1.1, ALv2 Future License (FSL-1.1-ALv2)

**Primary License:** This is a **Fair Source variant**, not Apache 2.0 or MIT initially.

**Usage Limitation:** The license restricts "Competing Use," defined as making the Software available commercially if it "substitutes for the Software" or "offers the same or substantially similar functionality." Permitted uses include internal use, non-commercial education, non-commercial research, and professional services.

**Key Feature:** It includes a future license grant: "We hereby irrevocably grant you an additional license to use the Software under the Apache License, Version 2.0 that is effective on the second anniversary of the date we make the Software available." After two years, the software automatically becomes available under Apache 2.0.

**Copyright:** 2022-2026 OpenAI

content.text: All facts verified. Compiling the findings report.

findings.title: gha-outrunner exists but is an extremely obscure single-maintainer project (3 stars, 0 forks)
findings.detail: VERIFIED: The project exists at https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner ('Ephemeral VM and container runners for GitHub Actions — no Kubernetes required'). It genuinely matches the design's assumptions: written in Go, MIT licensed, supports exactly the three backends the design names (Docker, libvirt/KVM qcow2, Tart on Apple Silicon), and registers via GitHub's scaleset API as an autoscaling runner group without Kubernetes. It is actively maintained — latest release v1.2.0 on April 20, 2026, 5 total releases, built by Pawel Subocz at Netwind. HOWEVER, at fetch time it has 3 stars, 0 forks, 17 open issues, and a single author. The entire gha-isolated design wraps this project as its execution engine, so the design inherits a severe bus-factor and abandonment risk from a project with essentially no community. The author did NOT confuse it with actions-runner-controller, rust-lang/gha-runner, omsf/gha-runner, or runs-on.com — those are distinct projects. Evidence: https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner
findings.recommendation: Acknowledge the dependency risk explicitly in the design. Options: (a) vendor/fork gha-outrunner since it is MIT, (b) build on GitHub's official standalone scale set client (https://github.com/actions/scaleset, public preview Feb 2026) as the durable API layer with gha-outrunner as reference code, or (c) contribute the wrapper features upstream to gha-outrunner instead of wrapping it.

findings.title: Design's assumed gha-outrunner interface (outrunner.yml, start/stop/status, count/backend/pids_limit keys) does not match the real tool
findings.detail: VERIFIED against the actual docs (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner/main/docs/reference/configuration.md and .../cli.md): (1) The config file is `config.yml` (default `/etc/outrunner/config.yml`), NOT `outrunner.yml` as src/config.rs writes. (2) There is no `backend:` key — each runner in the `runners:` map specifies exactly one of `docker:`, `libvirt:`, or `tart:` sub-blocks. (3) There is no `count:` key — concurrency is `max_runners` (default 2). (4) `memory` is an integer in MiB (default 8192), not '4G'/'4g' strings; there is no `resources:` block and no `pids_limit` option at all. (5) The CLI is a single foreground command with only --config/--url/--token/--max-runners flags — there are NO start/stop/status subcommands, so src/runner.rs's lifecycle model ('start/stop/status via gha-outrunner') is wrong; gha-isolated would have to manage the process itself (spawn/signal/pidfile or systemd unit). (6) Auth is PAT-only (token_file, --token, GITHUB_TOKEN, systemd-creds); no GitHub App auth is documented. (7) gha-outrunner has no built-in service installer, so the design's service.rs must write systemd/launchd units itself — consistent with the design but worth stating.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite src/config.rs to emit the real schema (config.yml with url, token_file, runners.<name>.{labels, docker|libvirt|tart, max_runners}, integer MiB memory) and redesign runner.rs as a process supervisor (spawn foreground outrunner, SIGINT for clean shutdown) rather than assuming subcommands. Drop pids_limit from the config or file an upstream feature request.

findings.title: GCE 'no strong KVM' claim is factually wrong — Compute Engine supports nested virtualization on most Intel x86 machine types
findings.detail: VERIFIED FALSE: Google Cloud docs (https://docs.cloud.google.com/compute/docs/instances/nested-virtualization/overview) state Compute Engine adds Intel VT-x to VMs so an L1 VM can run KVM. Requirements: Intel Haswell or later CPU platform; enabled per-VM with `--enable-nested-virtualization`; NOT supported on E2, memory-optimized, AMD-based, Arm-based, or H4D machine types. So on a correctly chosen GCE machine type (e.g. N1/N2/C2/C3 Intel), /dev/kvm exists and the design's libvirt backend works — the backend-selection table row 'Linux / GCE → No/weak KVM → Docker+Sysbox' is wrong as a blanket statement. Caveats that partially support the design's instinct: nested KVM has a real performance penalty (see https://piware.de/post/2026-02-13-google-cloud-platform-nested-virtualization-revisited/, Feb 2026), and Arm/AMD GCE instances genuinely have no nested virt. Note the design's own detection code (checking /dev/kvm) would actually pick libvirt on such GCE VMs, contradicting its own table.
findings.recommendation: Change the design text: GCE with Intel machine types + --enable-nested-virtualization is a first-class libvirt/KVM target; Docker+Sysbox is the fallback for E2/AMD/Arm instances or where nested-virt performance is unacceptable. Add a GCE helper that provisions VMs with the nested-virt flag and documents the machine-type constraint.

findings.title: The design largely reinvents existing wheels — gha-outrunner itself plus GARM, GitHub's official scaleset client, RunsOn, actuated, Ubicloud, WarpBuild
findings.detail: VERIFIED: The proposed gha-isolated adds only platform detection, backend selection, config generation, and service install on top of gha-outrunner — which already does the hard part (ephemeral runners, scaleset registration, Docker/libvirt/Tart isolation, no k8s: https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner). Established alternatives covering the same space: (1) GARM — Cloudbase's multi-cloud auto-scaling manager for GitHub Actions/Gitea self-hosted runners with pluggable providers, mature and widely used (https://github.com/cloudbase/garm). (2) GitHub's official standalone Runner Scale Set Client, actions/scaleset, public preview Feb 5 2026 — a Go module for building custom autoscalers on VMs/containers/bare metal WITHOUT Kubernetes (https://github.com/actions/scaleset, https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/186265). (3) Commercial/hosted: RunsOn (runs in your AWS account), actuated (microVMs on your hardware), Ubicloud, WarpBuild, Namespace, Blacksmith (https://runs-on.com/alternatives-to/github-actions-runners/, https://betterstack.com/community/comparisons/actuated-alternatives/). (4) ARC remains the k8s reference implementation (https://github.com/actions/actions-runner-controller). Cirrus Labs' own runner products are shutting down after the OpenAI acquisition (Cirrus CI ends June 1, 2026: https://www.warpbuild.com/blog/cirrus-ci-shutting-down). The unique value of gha-isolated is only the thin cross-platform 'pick the best backend automatically + install as a service' UX.
findings.recommendation: Before building, evaluate GARM (with libvirt/LXD providers) and gha-outrunner-as-is with a small install script. If proceeding, scope gha-isolated as a thin installer/supervisor and consider upstreaming auto-detection and service install to gha-outrunner (MIT, would likely welcome contributions) instead of a separate binary.

findings.title: 'GitHub Scale Sets' without Kubernetes is now legitimate — but the design should cite the official actions/scaleset client, not ARC concepts
findings.detail: VERIFIED FEASIBLE: Runner scale sets were historically an ARC/Kubernetes concept, but two things make the design's 'Scale Sets + ephemeral runners by default' claim valid without k8s as of 2026: (1) gha-outrunner already registers via the scaleset API as an autoscaling runner group (https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner), and (2) GitHub shipped an official standalone Go client for the Runner Scale Set APIs in public preview on Feb 5, 2026 (https://github.com/actions/scaleset; announcement https://github.blog/changelog/2026-02-05-github-actions-early-february-2026-updates/ and https://github.com/orgs/community/discussions/186265). GitHub explicitly positions it for VMs, bare metal, and non-k8s infrastructure; ARC remains the reference implementation. So no need to fall back to 'ephemeral runners + JIT config' wording — scale sets are correct — though JIT-config ephemeral runners remain a simpler alternative registration path.
findings.recommendation: Update the design to name the actual mechanism: 'runner scale set APIs via gha-outrunner (see GitHub's standalone actions/scaleset client)'. Note the scaleset client is public preview, so API stability caveats apply.

findings.title: Tart licensing resolved favorably: fees dropped after OpenAI acquisition, now FSL-1.1-ALv2 — but maintainer team has moved on
findings.detail: VERIFIED: Historically Tart was Fair Source with a free tier limited to 100 CPU cores per organization on server installations (personal workstations royalty-free; https://tart.run/licensing/, https://github.com/cirruslabs/tart/discussions/433). On April 7, 2026 Cirrus Labs announced it is joining OpenAI (https://cirruslabs.org/, https://macstadium.com/blog/cirrus-labs-is-joining-openai): they stopped charging licensing fees and promised to relicense Tart/Vetu/Orchard more permissively. I fetched the current LICENSE file (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/cirruslabs/tart/main/LICENSE): it is now the Functional Source License FSL-1.1-ALv2, copyright 2022-2026 OpenAI — internal use (which self-hosted CI runners are) is fully permitted with NO core limits; only 'Competing Use' (selling a substitute product) is restricted; each release auto-converts to Apache 2.0 after two years. So licensing is a non-issue for gha-isolated. The real risk is maintenance: the team that built Tart moved to other OpenAI projects, Cirrus CI shut down June 1 2026, and Cirrus Runners closed to new customers — Tart's future cadence is uncertain community maintenance.
findings.recommendation: Design can rely on Tart license-wise (FSL internal use, no fees, no core limits). Add a note that Tart is now community/OpenAI-orphan maintained post-acquisition and pin known-good Tart versions; keep libvirt-on-macOS or Apple Virtualization.framework alternatives on the radar.

findings.title: Sysbox-CE is maintained in 2026, but 'assume Sysbox is installed' plus distro/kernel friction is hand-wavy
findings.detail: VERIFIED: Docker acquired Nestybox (2022); Sysbox remains a community open-source project with Docker as main sponsor, support best-effort via GitHub/Slack (https://github.com/nestybox/sysbox). It is NOT EOL: v0.7.0 was released/deployed around March 2026 (https://github.com/nestybox/sysbox/releases). Sysbox-EE is dead as a standalone product (archived at https://github.com/docker-archive/nestybox.sysbox-ee; features folded into Docker Desktop). Friction points: distro/kernel sensitivity is real — e.g. open issue 'Sysbox 0.7.0 not working on ubuntu 26.04' (https://github.com/nestybox/sysbox/issues/1005); packaged installs target specific Ubuntu/Debian/kernel combos (https://github.com/nestybox/sysbox/blob/master/docs/user-guide/install-package.md). Note Sysbox requires no KVM, so it is a coherent fallback where nested virt is unavailable — but the skeleton's backend.rs comment 'assume Sysbox is installed or will be' contradicts the design's own 'Ensure prerequisites' step. Also note gha-outrunner's docker backend does not document Sysbox integration, so wiring runtime=sysbox-runc would need upstream support or docker default-runtime configuration outside gha-outrunner.
findings.recommendation: Make Sysbox presence an explicit detected capability (check for sysbox-runc runtime in `docker info`), fall back to plain Docker with limits when absent, and document supported distro/kernel matrix. Verify gha-outrunner can actually launch containers under the sysbox-runc runtime before promising it.

findings.title: serde_yaml = "0.9" pins a deprecated, archived crate
findings.detail: VERIFIED: dtolnay released serde_yaml 0.9.34+deprecated as the final version and archived the repo in March 2024 (https://github.com/dtolnay/serde-yaml/releases/tag/0.9.34, https://docs.rs/crate/serde_yaml/latest, https://users.rust-lang.org/t/serde-yaml-deprecation-alternatives/108868). There is no officially designated successor — the author points users at crates.io alternatives. Community options include serde_yaml_ng and serde_norway (saphyr-based forks); note that the 'serde_yml' fork has had community concerns about maintenance quality, so vet before adopting. The crate still compiles and works, so this is a hygiene issue, not a blocker — but a brand-new 2026 project should not start on an archived dependency. Given the tiny config surface, even hand-writing the YAML (as config.rs's format! string already does) or emitting JSON-compatible YAML via serde_json would avoid the dependency entirely.
findings.recommendation: Replace serde_yaml with a maintained YAML crate (serde_yaml_ng or serde_norway) for parsing gha-outrunner config, or avoid a YAML dependency altogether since the tool mostly generates (not parses) a small config file.

input.summary: Fact-checked all 7 external assumptions in the gha-isolated design with live web evidence. Headline results: (1) gha-outrunner DOES exist (https://github.com/NetwindHQ/gha-outrunner) and matches the design's functional assumptions exactly (Go, MIT, Docker/libvirt/Tart backends, GitHub scaleset API, no k8s, v1.2.0 Apr 2026) — but it has 3 stars, 0 forks, and one maintainer, so the design's foundation is a bus-factor-1 dependency (flagged critical per instructions). (2) The design's assumed interface is wrong in detail: real config is config.yml (not outrunner.yml) with docker:/libvirt:/tart: blocks, max_runners (no count:), integer-MiB memory, no pids_limit, PAT-only auth, and the CLI is a single foreground process with NO start/stop/status subcommands. (3) The 'GCE has no strong KVM' claim is false — GCE supports nested virtualization on Intel Haswell+ machine types (not E2/AMD/Arm), so libvirt works on GCE. (4) Scale sets without Kubernetes are now officially supported via GitHub's standalone actions/scaleset Go client (public preview Feb 2026), so the design's scale-set wording is valid. (5) Tart licensing resolved favorably: after Cirrus Labs joined OpenAI (Apr 2026), fees were dropped and the license is now FSL-1.1-ALv2 (internal use free, no core limits, Apache-2.0 after 2 years) — though the original team has moved on. (6) Sysbox-CE is maintained (v0.7.0, Mar 2026, Docker-sponsored community project); Sysbox-EE is dead. (7) serde_yaml is deprecated/archived with no official successor. Prior art is substantial (GARM, actions/scaleset, RunsOn, actuated, Ubicloud, WarpBuild, ARC): gha-isolated's unique value is only a thin auto-detect + service-install layer, which would arguably be better contributed upstream to gha-outrunner. Could not verify one item first-hand: the tart.run/licensing page fetch was permission-denied, so Tart's historical 100-core free tier is sourced from search results and GitHub discussion #433 rather than the page itself; the current license was verified directly from the repo's LICENSE file.
